title: runCERIS
text: R package implementing Critical Environmental Regressor Informed Search (CERIS) framework dissecting genotype--environment (G×E) interaction multi-environment trials (MET). Provides exhaustive window-scanning algorithm identify critical developmental periods environmental covariates maximally explain G×E variance, coupled Joint Regression Analysis (JRA), reaction norm–based genomic prediction (JGRA), three cross-validation schemes.

title: runCERIS
text: Language: R (>= 4.1.0), base R (tidyverse) Visualization: ggplot2 + patchwork (composite figures), pheatmap (dendrogram heatmaps), colorspace (perceptually uniform HCL palettes) Genomic prediction: rrBLUP (G-BLUP via ridge regression), optional BGLR (BayesB variable selection) Interactive: Shiny GUI (inst/shiny/) modular architecture Documentation: roxygen2 inline docs, pkgdown site (docs/), 10 vignettes Testing: testthat (edition 3)

title: runCERIS
text: Analytical pipeline: load_crop_data() → prepare_trait_data() → compute_env_means() → compute_window_params() → analysis (run_CERIS, jra_model, jgra, loocv, cv_*) → plot_*() visualization Canonical column names: line_code (genotype ID), env_code (environment ID), Yobs (observed phenotype), meanY (environment mean ȳ_j), kPara (best environmental covariate), DAP (days planting) CERIS algorithm: O(n²) exhaustive scan (DAP_start, DAP_end) pairs window ≥ 7 days; computes Pearson r −log₁₀(P) window-aggregated covariate ȳ_j environmental parameter Reaction norm model: y_ij = α_i + β_i × h_j + ε_ij, h_j environmental covariate (either ȳ_j JRA kPara CERIS-informed regression) JGRA: predicts α̂_i β̂_i SNP markers via G-BLUP BayesB; three validation schemes — RM.E (environment LOO), RM.G (genotype k-fold), RM.GE (combined) Genomic prediction: solve_gp() dispatches rrBLUP::mixed.solve() (closed-form) BGLR::BGLR() (MCMC); missing markers imputed column mean Progress callbacks: computationally intensive functions accept progress = function(fraction) Shiny progress bar integration Composite figures: multi-panel plots use patchwork operators (+ horizontal, / vertical, wrap_plots())

what: How the Exhaustive Window Search Works
title: CERIS Search
text: CERIS tests every possible contiguous window days planting (DAP). window defined start day (Day_x) end day (Day_y), Day_x <= Day_y. window environmental parameter, algorithm: Computes summary statistic (e.g., cumulative sum mean) parameter within window every environment. Correlates summary environmental trait mean across environments. Records Pearson correlation (R) p-value (P). nested loop (start, end) pairs creates triangular search space. result complete map trait–environment association changes developmental window, every candidate parameter.

title: CERIS Search
text: run_CERIS() performs exhaustive search. max_days argument limits maximum DAP consider, controls search space runtime. Setting max_days = 80 good starting point sorghum flowering time: loo parameter controls whether leave-one-cross-validation performed (see ). Setting progress = NULL suppresses progress bar, appropriate non-interactive use.
code: ceris_result <- run_CERIS(   env_mean_trait,   env_params,   params   = params,   max_days = 80,   loo      = FALSE,   progress = NULL )

title: CERIS Search
text: result data frame one row per (Day_x, Day_y) combination: columns : example, R_GDD correlation cumulative GDD within window trait environmental mean. High absolute correlations low p-values indicate parameter window strong predictor GxE.

title: CERIS Search
text: ceris_identify_best() scans full search result identifies window parameter strongest association. min_window argument sets minimum window length avoid spuriously narrow windows: returned list contains best parameter name, start end DAP optimal window, correlation value, negative log p-value. headline result CERIS analysis.
code: best <- ceris_identify_best(ceris_result, params = params, min_window = 7) best #> $param_name #> [1] "PTS" #>  #> $dap_start #> [1] 9 #>  #> $dap_end #> [1] 16 #>  #> $correlation #> [1] 0.9587 #>  #> $neg_log_p #> [1] 3.1866

title: CERIS Search
text: plot_ceris_heatmap() displays full search result set triangular heatmaps, one per parameter. x-axis window start day, y-axis window end day, color encodes correlation strength:  Look “hot spots” — regions consistently strong correlation. well- defined hot spot indicates robust critical window. Diffuse patterns suggest association spread across broader developmental period. plot also includes marginal traces showing best correlation varies window start end days individually.
code: plot_ceris_heatmap(ceris_result, params = params, max_days = 80)

title: CERIS Search
text: identified best window, use compute_window_params() calculate parameter summary window environment. adds kPara column environmental means data frame: kPara column contains computed value best parameter within identified window environment. environmental covariate best predicts trait variation.

title: CERIS Search
text: plot_trait_env_param() visualizes relationship trait environmental mean windowed parameter:  tight linear relationship confirms identified window parameter capture dominant environmental driver. Points deviate trend may indicate environments additional factors play.
code: env_colors <- setNames(   scales::hue_pal()(nrow(env_mean_trait)),   env_mean_trait$env_code )  plot_trait_env_param(   env_mean_enriched,   trait      = "FTdap",   kpara_name = best$param_name,   dap_start  = best$dap_start,   dap_end    = best$dap_end,   env_colors = env_colors )

title: CERIS Search
text: standard CERIS search uses environments compute correlations. assess robustness, can enable leave-one-(LOO) cross-validation setting loo = TRUE. LOO mode, environment held turn search repeated remaining environments. provides honest estimate predictive performance: LOO result structure standard result. can pass ceris_identify_best() visualization functions way: LOO result agrees full-data result (parameter, similar window), can confident finding robust driven single outlier environment.
code: ceris_loo <- run_CERIS(   env_mean_trait,   env_params,   params   = params,   max_days = 80,   loo      = TRUE,   progress = NULL ) best_loo <- ceris_identify_best(ceris_loo, params = params, min_window = 7) best_loo #> $param_name #> [1] "PTS" #>  #> $dap_start #> [1] 9 #>  #> $dap_end #> [1] 16 #>  #> $correlation #> [1] 0.9541 #>  #> $neg_log_p #> [1] 2.5064 plot_ceris_heatmap(ceris_loo, params = params, max_days = 80)

title: CERIS Search
text: CERIS search workflow : Run run_CERIS() compute correlations windows parameters. Use ceris_identify_best() find optimal window parameter. Visualize plot_ceris_heatmap() inspect full search landscape. Extract window parameter compute_window_params() plot trait–parameter relationship plot_trait_env_param(). Validate LOO (loo = TRUE) confirm robustness. systematic approach turns complex GxE problem interpretable result: specific environmental factor, specific developmental window, explains genotypes perform differently across environments.

title: Cross-Validation
text: Cross-validation (CV) assesses well model predicts unseen data. CERIS provides three CV strategies differ held prediction: addition, loocv() performs line-level leave-one-cross-validation, comparing predictions based environmental mean versus CERIS-derived kPara. vignette walks four approaches compares predictive accuracy.

title: Cross-Validation
text: Load sorghum dataset run full CERIS pipeline obtain environmental parameter (kPara): Run CERIS search max_days = 80 speed, compute window parameters:
code: library(runCERIS)  d <- load_crop_data("sorghum")  exp_trait <- prepare_trait_data(d$traits, "FTdap") env_mean_trait <- compute_env_means(exp_trait, d$env_meta) ceris_result <- run_CERIS(   env_mean_trait = env_mean_trait,   env_params     = d$env_params,   params         = c("DL", "GDD", "PTT", "PTR", "PTS"),   max_days       = 80,   loo            = FALSE,   progress       = NULL )  best <- ceris_identify_best(ceris_result, params = c("DL", "GDD", "PTT", "PTR", "PTS")) best #> $param_name #> [1] "PTS" #>  #> $dap_start #> [1] 9 #>  #> $dap_end #> [1] 16 #>  #> $correlation #> [1] 0.9587 #>  #> $neg_log_p #> [1] 3.1866  env_mean_trait <- compute_window_params(   env_mean_trait = env_mean_trait,   env_params     = d$env_params,   dap_start      = best$dap_start,   dap_end        = best$dap_end,   params         = best$param_name )

title: Cross-Validation
text: loocv() function holds one line time within environment predicts trait value using () environmental mean (b) CERIS-derived kPara regression. provides direct comparison two prediction approaches: result contains observed values (Obs_trait), predictions environmental mean (Prd_trait_mean), predictions kPara (Prd_trait_kPara), genotype’s overall mean (Line_mean). Visualize prediction accuracy plot_prediction_result():  four-panel plot compares observed versus predicted values prediction method, colored environment. Points closer diagonal indicate better prediction. kPara-based predictions typically show tighter clustering around diagonal CERIS search identified strong environmental driver.
code: loo_result <- loocv(exp_trait, env_mean_trait) head(loo_result) #>   env_code line_code Prd_trait_mean Prd_trait_kPara Obs_trait Line_mean #> 1     IA13       E10        109.455          94.242   89.7396  78.37150 #> 2     IA14       E10         89.676          91.970   77.3756  80.43217 #> 3     KS11       E10         84.764          93.348   95.5120  77.40943 #> 4     KS12       E10         90.948          97.146  107.9397  75.33815 #> 5     PR11       E10         65.257          62.074   53.4805  84.41468 #> 6     PR12       E10         65.317          59.643   53.1187  84.47498 plot_prediction_result(   obs_prd        = loo_result,   env_mean_trait = env_mean_trait,   trait          = "FTdap",   kpara_name     = best$param_name )

title: Cross-Validation
text: cv_env() function drops one environment time predicts trait values genotypes environment using remaining environments: row contains predicted value (Yprd), observed value (Yobs), held-environment (env_code), replicate indicator (Rep). Compute overall prediction accuracy:
code: cv_env_result <- cv_env(env_mean_trait, exp_trait) head(cv_env_result) #>   line_code env_code   Yprd    Yobs Rep #> 1       E10     PR12 69.260 53.1187   1 #> 2      E100     PR12 60.160 60.0257   1 #> 3      E101     PR12 42.997 56.9559   1 #> 4      E102     PR12 42.998 56.5722   1 #> 5      E103     PR12 48.881 53.8861   1 #> 6      E104     PR12 58.647 56.5722   1 cor(cv_env_result$Yprd, cv_env_result$Yobs) #> [1] 0.5689695

title: Cross-Validation
text: cv_genotype() function partitions genotypes K folds, holds fold turn, predicts trait values held-genotypes using genomic marker data. requires: slope-intercept matrix (lm_ab_matrix) slope_intercept() kPara-based coefficients. genotype marker matrix (SNPs) filtered lines present trial. Run K-fold CV gFold = 5 gIteration = 2 speed:
code: lm_ab <- slope_intercept(exp_trait, env_mean_trait, type = "kPara") SNPs <- prepare_genotype(d$genotype, unique(exp_trait$line_code)) cv_geno_result <- cv_genotype(   gFold          = 5,   gIteration     = 2,   SNPs           = SNPs,   lm_ab_matrix   = lm_ab,   env_mean_trait = env_mean_trait,   exp_trait      = exp_trait,   progress       = NULL ) head(cv_geno_result) #>   line_code env_code   Yprd    Yobs Rep #> 1      E102     PR12 55.898 56.5722   1 #> 2      E103     PR12 52.691 53.8861   1 #> 3      E107     PR12 52.999 58.1071   1 #> 4      E108     PR12 46.756 60.0257   1 #> 5      E114     PR12 48.203 54.6536   1 #> 6      E119     PR12 57.469 56.5722   1 cor(cv_geno_result$Yprd, cv_geno_result$Yobs) #> [1] 0.571371

title: Cross-Validation
text: cv_combined() function simultaneously holds environments genotypes, providing stringent test predictive ability:
code: cv_comb_result <- cv_combined(   gFold          = 5,   gIteration     = 2,   SNPs           = SNPs,   env_mean_trait = env_mean_trait,   exp_trait      = exp_trait,   progress       = NULL ) head(cv_comb_result) #>   line_code env_code   Yprd    Yobs Rep #> 1      E103     PR12 53.275 53.8861   1 #> 2      E106     PR12 51.051 57.3396   1 #> 3      E116     PR12 61.195 56.1885   1 #> 4       E12     PR12 56.464 56.1885   1 #> 5      E129     PR12 49.973 56.5722   1 #> 6       E13     PR12 63.522 58.8745   1 cor(cv_comb_result$Yprd, cv_comb_result$Yobs) #> [1] 0.5712037

title: Cross-Validation
text: Use plot_cv_results() display three CV methods side side. function takes list result data frames vector labels:  panel shows observed versus predicted values one CV method, points colored environment correlation coefficient displayed. Typically, cv_env (dropping environments) yields highest accuracy retains genotype information, cv_combined (dropping ) challenging scenario.
code: plot_cv_results(   cv_results = list(cv_env_result, cv_geno_result, cv_comb_result),   labels     = c("1-to-2 (Env)", "1-to-3 (Geno)", "1-to-4 (Both)") )

title: Cross-Validation
text: gap cv_env cv_combined reflects much predictive power comes knowing genotype versus knowing environment. gap small, CERIS-derived environmental parameter captures GxE variation, making environmental characterization dominant source prediction accuracy.

what: Fetching weather data from NASA POWER
title: Data Format Reference
text: easiest way build env_params trials use fetch_nasa_power(). function downloads daily temperature data NASA POWER API automatically computes CERIS-derived parameters (DL, GDD, PTT, PTR, PTD1, PTD2, PTS). need env_meta data frame latitude, longitude, planting date environment: function fetches T2M_MAX T2M_MIN NASA POWER, computes day length astronomically latitude day year, derives photothermal parameters. API key required. See ?fetch_nasa_power details.
code: # Your env_meta must have: env_code, lat, lon, PlantingDate env_params <- fetch_nasa_power(d$env_meta, max_dap = 120, base_temp = 10) head(env_params)  # The result is ready to use in run_CERIS() ceris_result <- run_CERIS(   env_mean_trait, env_params,   params = c("DL", "GDD", "PTT", "PTR", "PTS"),   max_days = 80 )

title: Data Format Reference
text: already daily weather records (temperature, day length), can compute derived parameters assemble env_params data frame manually. compile_envirome() function can help data stored tab-separated files disk — see ?compile_envirome details. minimal workflow construct env_params hand:
code: # Suppose you have a data frame 'weather' with columns: #   env_code, DAP, Tmax, Tmin, DL, PRECIP  weather$GDD <- pmax(0, (weather$Tmax + weather$Tmin) / 2 - 10) weather$PTT <- weather$GDD * weather$DL weather$PTR <- weather$GDD / weather$DL weather$PTS <- (weather$Tmax^2 - weather$Tmin^2) * (weather$DL)^2  env_params <- weather[, c("env_code", "DAP", "DL", "GDD",                            "PTT", "PTR", "PTS")]

title: Data Format Reference
text: env_code mismatches: Environment codes must identical across traits, env_meta, env_params. Check trailing whitespace inconsistent formatting (e.g., “Env1” vs “ENV1”). Missing DAP values: env_params continuous DAP sequences within environment (1, 2, 3, …, N). Gaps cause errors window search. Non-numeric trait columns: Trait columns must numeric. contain text (e.g., “NA” string), convert analysis. Genotype matrix orientation: Rows must lines columns must markers. matrix transposed, prepare_genotype() auto-detect fix . Marker coding: genotype matrix contain numeric values (typically 0, 1, 2 biallelic markers). Factor character columns cause errors.

title: Genomic Prediction (JGRA)
text: Genomic prediction integrates marker data environmental covariates predict genotype performance untested environments. CERIS provides two complementary approaches JGRA (Joint Genomic Regression Analysis) framework: Reaction norm approach (jgra()): Models genotype--environment interaction function genomic breeding values interaction environmental covariate. reaction norm captures genotype’s performance changes along environmental gradient identified CERIS. Marker effect approach (jgra_marker()): Estimates marker-specific sensitivity environmental covariate. Rather summarizing genomic information single breeding value, approach models individual marker effects interactions environment. methods leverage CERIS-identified critical environmental parameter (kPara) define environmental gradient, replacing traditional environmental mean biologically interpretable covariate. vignette demonstrates : Prepare required inputs JGRA standard CERIS pipeline. Run genomic prediction using reaction norm marker effect approaches. Evaluate prediction accuracy different cross-validation schemes. Forecast performance new environments using forecast_next_year().

title: Genomic Prediction (JGRA)
text: Load sorghum dataset run standard CERIS pipeline compute_window_params(). produces kPara column needed JGRA. Run CERIS exhaustive search identify best environmental window: Compute window-summarized environmental parameter environment:
code: library(runCERIS)  d <- load_crop_data("sorghum")  exp_trait <- prepare_trait_data(d$traits, "FTdap") env_mean_trait <- compute_env_means(exp_trait, d$env_meta) params <- c("DL", "GDD", "PTT", "PTR", "PTS")  ceris_result <- run_CERIS(   env_mean_trait = env_mean_trait,   env_params     = d$env_params,   params         = params,   max_days       = 80,   loo            = FALSE,   progress       = NULL )  best <- ceris_identify_best(ceris_result, params = params) best #> $param_name #> [1] "PTS" #>  #> $dap_start #> [1] 9 #>  #> $dap_end #> [1] 16 #>  #> $correlation #> [1] 0.9587 #>  #> $neg_log_p #> [1] 3.1866 env_mean_trait <- compute_window_params(   env_mean_trait = env_mean_trait,   env_params     = d$env_params,   dap_start      = best$dap_start,   dap_end        = best$dap_end,   params         = best$param_name ) head(env_mean_trait) #>   env_code    meanY env_notes     lat      lon PlantingDate TrialYear Location #> 1     PR12 56.77317         2 18.0373 -66.7963   2011-12-12      2011       PR #> 2     PR11 56.85371         1 18.0373 -66.7963   2010-12-04      2010       PR #> 3    PR14S 60.45186         7 18.0373 -66.7963   2014-06-05      2014       PR #> 4     KS11 73.81378         3 39.1836 -96.5717   2011-06-08      2011       KS #> 5     IA14 74.44027         6 42.0308 -93.6319   2014-06-10      2014       IA #> 6     KS12 80.02100         4 39.1836 -96.5717   2012-06-07      2012       KS #>      kPara #> 1 309321.4 #> 2 335746.3 #> 3 437819.6 #> 4 804678.1 #> 5 742952.3 #> 6 900995.8

title: Genomic Prediction (JGRA)
text: jgra() jgra_marker() functions require three primary inputs: pheno: wide-format data frame line_code first column one column per environment containing trait values. output prepare_line_by_env(). geno: data frame line_code first column followed marker columns. must constructed genotype matrix. envir: data frame env_code environmental parameter column use covariate. envir data frame links environment kPara value — CERIS-identified environmental covariate summarized critical window. replaces traditional environmental mean parameter direct biological interpretation (e.g., cumulative photoperiod sensitive developmental period).
code: # Wide-format phenotype matrix pheno <- prepare_line_by_env(exp_trait, env_mean_trait) head(pheno[, 1:4]) #>   line_code    PR12    PR11   PR14S #> 1       E10 53.1187 53.4805 82.8025 #> 2      E100 60.0257 66.6417 64.0564 #> 3      E101 56.9559 58.7449 65.3437 #> 4      E102 56.5722 54.3579 60.0090 #> 5      E103 53.8861 54.7966 77.9528 #> 6      E104 56.5722 53.4805 64.8587  # Genotype data frame with line_code as first column geno_df <- data.frame(   line_code = rownames(d$genotype),   d$genotype,   check.names = FALSE ) geno_df[1:5, 1:5] #>             line_code E5 E6 E7 E8 #> S1_1857181 S1_1857181  1  1 -1  1 #> S1_1857180 S1_1857180  1  1 -1  1 #> S1_1857182 S1_1857182  1  1 -1  1 #> S1_1857183 S1_1857183  1  1 -1  1 #> S1_1857184 S1_1857184  1  1 -1  1  # Environmental covariate envir <- env_mean_trait[, c("env_code", "kPara")] envir #>   env_code    kPara #> 1     PR12 309321.4 #> 2     PR11 335746.3 #> 3    PR14S 437819.6 #> 4     KS11 804678.1 #> 5     IA14 742952.3 #> 6     KS12 900995.8 #> 7     IA13 795978.4

title: Genomic Prediction (JGRA)
text: jgra() function fits reaction norm model decomposes genotype performance main genetic effect (breeding value) genotype-specific sensitivity environmental covariate. method argument controls cross-validation scheme: "RM.E": Leave-one-environment-— predicts performance environments excluded training. "RM.G": K-fold genotype cross-validation — predicts performance genotypes excluded training. "RM.GE": Combined — simultaneously excludes genotypes environments. start environment leave-one-(RM.E), using fold = 5 reshuffle = 2 faster computation vignette: result contains prediction accuracy metrics: r_within value measures well model predicts genotype rankings within environment, r_across captures prediction accuracy across observations. predictions data frame contains observed (obs) predicted (pre) values along environment identifiers.
code: jgra_result <- jgra(   pheno    = pheno,   geno     = geno_df,   envir    = envir,   enp      = "kPara",   env_meta = d$env_meta,   method   = "RM.E",   fold     = 5,   reshuffle = 2,   progress = NULL ) # Within-environment correlation jgra_result$r_within #>    cor_within envir #> 1 -0.09051804  PR12 #> 2 -0.20731434  PR11 #> 3  0.84652584 PR14S #> 4  0.57745974  KS11 #> 5  0.44517081  IA14 #> 6  0.85891930  KS12 #> 7  0.82833064  IA13  # Across-environment correlation jgra_result$r_across #> [1] 0.3274092  # Prediction data frame head(jgra_result$predictions) #>       obs      pre envir #> 1 53.1187 72.18935  PR12 #> 2 60.0257 67.23481  PR12 #> 3 56.9559 68.09015  PR12 #> 4 56.5722 62.18671  PR12 #> 5 53.8861 84.15024  PR12 #> 6 56.5722 78.80609  PR12

title: Genomic Prediction (JGRA)
text: jgra_marker() function uses interface estimates marker-specific environmental sensitivities rather summarizing genomic information single breeding value. approach can capture complex patterns GxE individual markers differential sensitivity environmental covariate: Compare marker effect results reaction norm approach: Note: jgra_marker() requires datasets sufficient genotype matrix dimensions. sorghum example dataset small marker effect method. larger datasets (e.g., hundreds lines thousands markers), methods run successfully can directly compared. many cases two methods yield similar accuracy environment-level prediction (RM.E), marker effect approach may outperform reaction norm model predicting untested genotypes (RM.G RM.GE), since models marker-level heterogeneity environmental sensitivity.
code: marker_result <- jgra_marker(   pheno    = pheno,   geno     = geno_df,   envir    = envir,   enp      = "kPara",   env_meta = d$env_meta,   method   = "RM.E",   fold     = 5,   reshuffle = 2,   progress = NULL ) comparison <- data.frame(   Method    = c("Reaction Norm (jgra)", "Marker Effects (jgra_marker)"),   r_within  = c(jgra_result$r_within, marker_result$r_within),   r_across  = c(jgra_result$r_across, marker_result$r_across) ) comparison

title: Genomic Prediction (JGRA)
text: forecast_next_year() function provides direct way evaluate well model predicts performance environments excluded training. simulates practical breeding scenario historical data existing trial sites used predict outcomes new locations future years. use first five sorghum environments training hold remaining environments test set: Visualize forecast results plot_prediction_result(), produces four-panel display showing observed versus predicted values, prediction environment, relationship kPara:  top-left panel shows observed versus predicted trait values colored environment. top-right panel displays predictions environmental mean. bottom panels show relationship CERIS-identified environmental parameter. Points close diagonal indicate accurate predictions.
code: train_envs <- env_mean_trait$env_code[1:5] test_envs  <- setdiff(env_mean_trait$env_code, train_envs)  cat("Training environments:", paste(train_envs, collapse = ", "), "\n") #> Training environments: PR12, PR11, PR14S, KS11, IA14 cat("Test environments:    ", paste(test_envs, collapse = ", "), "\n") #> Test environments:     KS12, IA13  forecast_result <- forecast_next_year(   exp_trait      = exp_trait,   env_mean_trait = env_mean_trait,   trn_env        = train_envs ) plot_prediction_result(   obs_prd        = forecast_result,   env_mean_trait = env_mean_trait,   trait          = "FTdap",   kpara_name     = best$param_name )

title: Genomic Prediction (JGRA)
text: jgra() jgra_marker() accept gp_method parameter switch rrBLUP (default, ridge regression BLUP) BayesB (Bayesian variable selection via BGLR). BayesB assigns marker-specific shrinkage, allowing markers large effects shrinking others near zero — useful trait architecture involves large-effect QTL. use BayesB, install BGLR package set gp_method = "BayesB": BayesB computationally expensive rrBLUP due MCMC sampling. exploratory analyses, reduce nIter burnIn. publication-quality results, use nIter >= 10000 burnIn >= 2000. seed parameter ensures reproducible results across runs — applies BayesB MCMC sampling random fold assignments used cross-validation. gp_method seed parameters available cv_genotype() cv_combined():
code: # install.packages("BGLR")  jgra_bayesb <- jgra(   pheno     = pheno,   geno      = geno_df,   envir     = envir,   enp       = "kPara",   env_meta  = d$env_meta,   method    = "RM.G",   gp_method = "BayesB",   nIter     = 5000,   burnIn    = 1000,   seed      = 42,   fold      = 5,   reshuffle = 2 ) jgra_bayesb$r_across cv_result <- cv_genotype(   gFold = 5, gIteration = 2,   SNPs = SNPs, lm_ab_matrix = lm_ab,   env_mean_trait = env_mean_trait, exp_trait = exp_trait,   gp_method = "BayesB", nIter = 5000, burnIn = 1000, seed = 42 )

title: Getting Started with CERIS
text: CERIS (Critical Environmental Regressor Informed Search) R package dissecting genotype--environment (GxE) interactions exhaustive environmental window analysis. multi-environment trials, crop performance varies across locations years. CERIS helps answer fundamental question: environmental factor, developmental window, best explains observed variation trait across environments? core idea straightforward. every possible contiguous window days planting (defined start day end day), CERIS computes summary environmental parameter (e.g., cumulative growing degree days, mean photoperiod) correlates summary trait’s environmental mean. exhaustively searching windows parameters, package identifies critical developmental period environmental driver strongly associated GxE variation.

title: Getting Started with CERIS
text: CERIS JGRA (Joint Genomic Regression Analysis) methods originally developed Dr. Jianming Yu’s lab Iowa State University, key contributions Xianran Li, Tingting Guo, collaborators. foundational work demonstrated environmental indices identified exhaustive search can used model phenotypic plasticity enable genomic prediction across environments major crops including sorghum, maize, wheat, oat, rice (Li et al. 2018; Li et al. 2021; Guo et al. 2024; Wei et al. 2025). original code available github.com/jmyu/CERIS_JGRA. R package developed Yu-Ru Chen, adapted restructured original functions documented, tested, user-friendly R package. researcher frequently applies methods, Yu-Ru Chen recognized packaging code make far accessible plant breeding community. Beyond providing convenient interface, package aims help users understand deeper quantitative genetics concepts behind GxE analysis — reaction norms Finlay-Wilkinson regression genomic prediction environmental covariates.

title: Statistical Foundations of Joint Regression Analysis
text: multi-environment trials (MET), phenotypic value yijy_{ij} genotype ii environment jj can decomposed : yij=μ+gi+ej+(ge)ij+εijy_{ij} = \mu + g_i + e_j + (ge)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ij} μ\mu grand mean, gig_i genotypic main effect, eje_j environmental main effect, (ge)ij(ge)_{ij} genotype--environment interaction, εij\varepsilon_{ij} residual error. interaction term (ge)ij(ge)_{ij} central challenge: causes genotype rankings change across environments, complicating selection decisions. Joint Regression Analysis (JRA) models interaction structured, linear function environmental index.

title: Statistical Foundations of Joint Regression Analysis
text: Finlay Wilkinson (1963) proposed regressing genotype’s performance environmental mean y‾⋅j\bar{y}_{\cdot j}: yij=αi+βiy‾⋅j+δijy_{ij} = \alpha_i + \beta_i \, \bar{y}_{\cdot j} + \delta_{ij} : αi\alpha_i intercept genotype ii (expected performance y‾⋅j=0\bar{y}_{\cdot j} = 0) βi\beta_i regression coefficient (sensitivity environmental quality) δij\delta_{ij} deviation regression (residual G×\timesE explained linear model) environmental index y‾⋅j=n−1∑=1nyij\bar{y}_{\cdot j} = n^{-1} \sum_{=1}^{n} y_{ij} marginal mean genotypes environment jj, serving composite measure environmental quality.

title: Statistical Foundations of Joint Regression Analysis
text: raw intercept αi\alpha_i depends origin environmental index scale, often interpretable report adjusted intercept evaluated grand mean: α̂*=α̂+β̂iy‾‾\hat{\alpha}_i^{*} = \hat{\alpha}_i + \hat{\beta}_i \, \bar{\bar{y}} y‾‾\bar{\bar{y}} mean environmental index. gives genotype’s expected phenotype average environmental conditions. runCERIS, Intcp_mean column returned jra_model() corresponds α̂*\hat{\alpha}_i^{*}.

title: Statistical Foundations of Joint Regression Analysis
text: validity JRA rests several assumptions practitioners verify:

title: Statistical Foundations of Joint Regression Analysis
text: model assumes genotype’s response environmental quality linear. practice, first-order approximation; curvilinear responses (e.g., optimum-type curves temperature) produce poor fits. Diagnostic: inspect per-genotype R2R^2 distribution. substantial fraction genotypes R2<0.5R^2 < 0.5, linear assumption may inadequate.
code: library(runCERIS)  d <- load_crop_data("sorghum") exp_trait <- prepare_trait_data(d$traits, "FTdap") env_mean_trait <- compute_env_means(exp_trait, d$env_meta) line_by_env <- prepare_line_by_env(exp_trait, env_mean_trait)  jra_result <- jra_model(line_by_env, env_mean_trait)  # Proportion of genotypes with adequate linear fit mean(as.numeric(jra_result$R2_mean) > 0.5) #> [1] 0.8734177

title: Statistical Foundations of Joint Regression Analysis
text: subtle important issue: y‾⋅j\bar{y}_{\cdot j} computed data used regression, creating dependency predictor response. Freeman Perkins (1971) showed leads biased slope estimates, particularly number genotypes small (n<20n < 20). large panels (n>100n > 100), bias negligible genotype’s contribution y‾⋅j\bar{y}_{\cdot j} small.

title: Statistical Foundations of Joint Regression Analysis
text: regression fitted across environments, statistical power depends directly number environments JJ. fewer 5–6 environments, slope estimates large standard errors R2^2 values unreliable. sorghum dataset runCERIS 7 environments, practical minimum.

title: Statistical Foundations of Joint Regression Analysis
text: Missing combinations (genotype observed certain environments) tolerated regression, missingness informative (e.g., genotype fails stress environments), estimated slope biased toward mean.

title: Statistical Foundations of Joint Regression Analysis
text: slope β̂\hat{\beta}_i adjusted intercept α̂*\hat{\alpha}_i^{*} together define genotype’s adaptation profile:  four quadrants distinct breeding implications: High intercept, slope ≈\approx 1: broadly adapted; suitable diverse target environments. High intercept, slope > 1: exploits favourable environments; ideal high-input systems risky stress. Low intercept, slope < 1: consistently poor; generally discarded. Low intercept, slope > 1: highly variable; may carry useful stress tolerance alleles detectable JGRA marker analysis.

# Compute adjusted intercept at grand mean grand_mean <- mean(env_mean_trait$meanY)  ggplot2::ggplot(   jra_result,   ggplot2::aes(     x = as.numeric(.data$Slope_mean),     y = as.numeric(.data$Intcp_mean)   ) ) +   ggplot2::geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 1.5) +   ggplot2::geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +   ggplot2::geom_hline(     yintercept = grand_mean,     linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50"   ) +   ggplot2::annotate("text", x = 0.55, y = max(as.numeric(jra_result$Intcp_mean)),                     label = "Stable & high
(broad adaptation)",                     size = 2.8, hjust = 0, color = "grey30") +   ggplot2::annotate("text", x = 1.3, y = max(as.numeric(jra_result$Intcp_mean)),                     label = "Responsive & high
(favourable env.)",                     size = 2.8, hjust = 1, color = "grey30") +   ggplot2::annotate("text", x = 0.55, y = min(as.numeric(jra_result$Intcp_mean)),                     label = "Stable & low
(poor overall)",                     size = 2.8, hjust = 0, color = "grey30") +   ggplot2::annotate("text", x = 1.3, y = min(as.numeric(jra_result$Intcp_mean)),                     label = "Responsive & low
(stress-prone)",                     size = 2.8, hjust = 1, color = "grey30") +   ggplot2::labs(     x = expression(paste("Slope ", hat(beta)[i])),     y = expression(paste("Adjusted intercept ", hat(alpha)[i]^"*"))   ) +   ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

title: Statistical Foundations of Joint Regression Analysis
text: deviation mean square sdi2s_{d_i}^2, introduced Eberhart Russell (1966), quantifies much genotype’s G×\timesE interaction captured linear model: sdi2=1J−2∑j=1Jδ̂ij2s_{d_i}^2 = \frac{1}{J - 2} \sum_{j=1}^{J} \hat{\delta}_{ij}^2 genotype high R2R^2 low sdi2s_{d_i}^2 well described linear model. Conversely, genotype low R2R^2 exhibits non-linear G×\timesE warrants investigation (e.g., via AMMI GGE biplot analysis).  right panel, distribution R2R^2 values across genotypes indicates overall adequacy linear model. left-skewed distribution (R2>0.7R^2 > 0.7) supports JRA; uniform right-skewed distribution suggests non-linear methods may needed.
code: plot_jra(exp_trait, env_mean_trait, jra_result, trait = "FTdap")

title: Statistical Foundations of Joint Regression Analysis
text: classical JRA uses y‾⋅j\bar{y}_{\cdot j} sole environmental index. two fundamental limitations: Circularity: predictor derived response variable, inflating correlations preventing true --sample prediction. Biological opacity: y‾⋅j\bar{y}_{\cdot j} conflates environmental drivers (temperature, photoperiod, moisture, soil) single number, preventing mechanistic interpretation. limitations motivate CERIS framework.

title: Statistical Foundations of Joint Regression Analysis
text: CERIS replaces environmental mean biophysically meaningful covariate hjh_j aggregated critical developmental window: yij=αi+βihj+δijy_{ij} = \alpha_i + \beta_i \, h_j + \delta_{ij} hjh_j (example) mean photoperiod DAP 20 55 environment jj. substitution several advantages: Predictive power: hjh_j can computed new environments using meteorological data, enabling true forecasting without observed phenotypes. Biological interpretation: slope βi\beta_i now quantifies genotype’s sensitivity specific environmental factor specific developmental stage. Higher R2R^2: correct factor window identified, covariate-based regression typically explains G×\timesE variance environmental mean.
code: params <- c("DL", "GDD", "PTT", "PTR", "PTS") ceris_result <- run_CERIS(env_mean_trait, d$env_params, params, max_days = 80) best <- ceris_identify_best(ceris_result, params = params)  env_mean_trait <- compute_window_params(   env_mean_trait, d$env_params,   best$dap_start, best$dap_end, best$param_name )  si_both <- slope_intercept(exp_trait, env_mean_trait, type = "both")  # Compare R-squared: environmental mean vs CERIS parameter r2_comparison <- data.frame(   Predictor = c("Environmental mean", "CERIS kPara"),   Median_R2 = c(     median(as.numeric(si_both$R2_para), na.rm = TRUE),     median(as.numeric(si_both$R2_para), na.rm = TRUE)   ) ) # Median R2 using kPara median(as.numeric(si_both$R2_para), na.rm = TRUE) #> [1] 0.7189

title: Statistical Foundations of Joint Regression Analysis
text: recommended workflow JRA within runCERIS framework: Fit classical JRA jra_model() establish baseline. Inspect R2R^2 distribution — median R2>0.7R^2 > 0.7, linear model adequate; otherwise consider non-linear alternatives. Run CERIS search run_CERIS() identify best environmental parameter developmental window. Re-fit kPara using slope_intercept(type = "") compare covariate-based regression mean-based baseline. Interpret slopes biologically terms identified environmental factor (e.g., photoperiod sensitivity vegetative phase). Validate leave-one-environment-cross-validation via loocv() confirm improved fit translates predictive accuracy.

title: Statistical Foundations of Joint Regression Analysis

title: Joint Regression Analysis
text: Joint Regression Analysis (JRA) classical method characterizing genotype--environment (GxE) interaction. Originally proposed Finlay Wilkinson (1963) refined Eberhart Russell (1966), JRA regresses genotype’s performance environmental mean. resulting slope intercept summarize genotype responds environmental quality.

title: Joint Regression Analysis
text: observed phenotype genotype ii environment jj modeled : yij=αi+βiy‾j+εijy_{ij} = \alpha_i + \beta_i \, \bar{y}_j + \varepsilon_{ij} : yijy_{ij} observed trait value genotype ii environment jj αi\alpha_i intercept (baseline performance) genotype ii βi\beta_i regression slope (sensitivity) genotype ii y‾j=1n∑=1nyij\bar{y}_j = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{=1}^{n} y_{ij} environmental mean (environmental index) εij\varepsilon_{ij} residual error environmental index y‾j\bar{y}_j serves proxy overall environmental quality — higher values indicate favorable growing conditions. genotype’s slope βi\beta_i quantifies responsiveness environmental variation, intercept αi\alpha_i reflects general performance level. CERIS implements JRA jra_model() function. vignette demonstrates fit model, visualize results, interpret output.

text: plot_jra() function produces two-panel display: Left panel: Regression lines genotypes plotted environmental mean, observed data points. Right panel: Histogram R-squared values across genotypes.  left panel, spread regression lines shows degree GxE interaction. Parallel lines indicate crossover interaction (genotype rankings remain constant), crossing lines indicate rank changes across environments. Genotypes low R-squared may exhibit non-linear responses influenced factors captured environmental mean.

title: Joint Regression Analysis
text: slope JRA direct biological interpretation: Breeders targeting broad adaptation prefer genotypes high intercepts (good overall performance) slopes near 1. targeting specific environments may select responsive genotypes (slope > 1) favorable conditions stable genotypes (slope < 1) stress-prone regions.

title: Joint Regression Analysis

title: Shiny Application Guide
text: CERIS Shiny application provides interactive interface complete analysis pipeline: Data: Load built-custom datasets. Explore: Visualize geographic patterns, trait distributions, environmental profiles. CERIS: Run exhaustive window search identify critical environmental factor. Reaction Norm: Examine genotype-specific environmental responses. JRA: Fit joint regression models. CV: Evaluate prediction accuracy cross-validation. JGRA: Perform genomic prediction (genotype data available). Visualization: Export plots result tables. reproducible analyses scripting, app functionality available CERIS R functions documented package vignettes.

what: Critical Environmental Regressor through Informed Search
title: Critical Environmental Regressor through Informed Search
text: Critical Environmental Regressor Informed Search runCERIS identifies critical environmental windows covariates driving genotype--environment (GxE) interaction multi-environment trials. performs exhaustive search possible day--planting (DAP) windows, correlating environmental parameters trait means find time period covariate predictive GxE variation. R package developed Dr. Yu-Ru Chen Iowa State University, adapting restructuring original CERIS-JGRA functions created Dr. Jianming Yu’s lab ISU. original code available github.com/jmyu/CERIS_JGRA. packaging methods documented, tested R package Shiny interface, runCERIS aims make GxE environmental window analysis accessible broader plant breeding community help users understand quantitative genetics concepts underlying genotype--environment interaction.

title: Critical Environmental Regressor through Informed Search
code: library(runCERIS)  # Load sorghum dataset d <- load_crop_data("sorghum")  # Prepare trait data (flowering time in DAP) exp_trait <- prepare_trait_data(d$traits, "FTdap")  # Compute environmental means and order environments env_mean_trait <- compute_env_means(exp_trait, d$env_meta) head(env_mean_trait) #>   env_code    meanY env_notes     lat      lon PlantingDate TrialYear Location #> 1     PR12 56.77317         2 18.0373 -66.7963   2011-12-12      2011       PR #> 2     PR11 56.85371         1 18.0373 -66.7963   2010-12-04      2010       PR #> 3    PR14S 60.45186         7 18.0373 -66.7963   2014-06-05      2014       PR #> 4     KS11 73.81378         3 39.1836 -96.5717   2011-06-08      2011       KS #> 5     IA14 74.44027         6 42.0308 -93.6319   2014-06-10      2014       IA #> 6     KS12 80.02100         4 39.1836 -96.5717   2012-06-07      2012       KS # Run CERIS search (use max_days = 80 for speed) params <- c("DL", "GDD", "PTT", "PTR", "PTS") ceris_result <- run_CERIS(env_mean_trait, d$env_params, params, max_days = 80)  # Find the best environmental window best <- ceris_identify_best(ceris_result, params) cat(sprintf("Best parameter: %s (DAP %d-%d, r = %.3f)\n",             best$param_name, best$dap_start, best$dap_end, best$correlation)) #> Best parameter: PTS (DAP 9-16, r = 0.959)

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validated Correlation — ceris_loo_cor
text: Computes leave-one-environment-(LOO) correlations trait vector one environmental parameter vectors. environment left , Pearson correlation \(-\log_{10}(p)\) computed remaining environments. per-fold values summarised across folds using user-chosen function (default: median).

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validated Correlation — ceris_loo_cor
code: ceris_loo_cor(trait, params_matrix, summary_fn = median)

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validated Correlation — ceris_loo_cor
text: trait Numeric vector trait means (one value per environment). params_matrix Numeric matrix environmental parameter values rows corresponding environments columns parameters. summary_fn Function used summarise LOO correlations across environments. Default median (robust outlier environments, original CERIS implementation). Use mean compare.

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validated Correlation — ceris_loo_cor
text: list components: r Summarised correlation parameter (length = ncol(params_matrix)). p Summarised \(-\log_{10}(p)\) parameter. r_matrix Matrix per-fold correlations (environments x parameters). p_matrix Matrix per-fold \(-\log_{10}(p)\) values.

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validated Correlation — ceris_loo_cor
text: function used internally run_CERIS loo = TRUE, can also called directly evaluate specific trait–parameter combination without running full exhaustive search.

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validated Correlation — ceris_loo_cor
code: # \donttest{ d <- load_crop_data("sorghum") exp_trait <- prepare_trait_data(d$traits, "FTdap") env_mean_trait <- compute_env_means(exp_trait, d$env_meta) params <- c("DL", "GDD", "PTT")  # Compute parameter means for a specific window (DAP 20-60) param_mat <- matrix(nrow = nrow(env_mean_trait), ncol = length(params)) for (i in seq_len(nrow(env_mean_trait))) {   e <- env_mean_trait$env_code[i]   e_data <- d$env_params[d$env_params$env_code == e, ]   rows <- e_data$DAP >= 20 & e_data$DAP <= 60   param_mat[i, ] <- colMeans(e_data[rows, params, drop = FALSE], na.rm = TRUE) }  loo <- ceris_loo_cor(env_mean_trait$meanY, param_mat) loo$r #> [1]  0.9389 -0.0867  0.4305 loo$p #> [1] 2.2613 0.0862 0.4044  # Use mean instead of median loo_mean <- ceris_loo_cor(env_mean_trait$meanY, param_mat, summary_fn = mean) loo_mean$r #> [1]  0.9368 -0.0182  0.4433 # }

title: Fetch Environmental Data from NASA POWER and Compute CERIS Parameters — fetch_nasa_power

Default 150. base_temp Numeric. Base temperature (Celsius) GDD calculation. Default 10. progress Logical. TRUE, print progress messages environment. Default TRUE.

title: Fetch Environmental Data from NASA POWER and Compute CERIS Parameters — fetch_nasa_power
text: data frame columns: env_code, DAP,   TMAX, TMIN, DL, GDD, PTT,   PTR, PTD1, PTD2, PTS.

title: Fetch Environmental Data from NASA POWER and Compute CERIS Parameters — fetch_nasa_power
text: returned data frame structure env_params can used directly run_CERIS. function calls NASA POWER daily API (https://power.larc.nasa.gov) retrieve T2M_MAX (daily maximum temperature) T2M_MIN (daily minimum temperature) agroclimatology community. Day length computed astronomically latitude day year using CBM model. API key required. Derived parameters follow definitions Tibbs-Cortes et al. (2024): DL Astronomical day length hours GDD max(0, (TMAX + TMIN) / 2 - base_temp) PTT GDD * DL PTR GDD / DL PTD1 (TMAX - TMIN) * DL PTD2 (TMAX - TMIN) / DL PTS (TMAX^2 - TMIN^2) * DL^2

text: pheno Wide-format phenotype data.frame (line_code + environment columns) geno Genotype data.frame matrix line_code identifier envir Data.frame env_code environmental parameter columns enp Name index environmental parameter column use env_meta Data.frame env_code (used filtering) tt_line Minimum non-NA lines per environment; environments fewer dropped tt_env Minimum non-NA environments per line; lines fewer dropped method One "RM.E" (env LOO), "RM.G" (genotype CV), "RM.GE" (combined) gp_method Genomic prediction method: "rrBLUP" (default, ridge regression BLUP) "BayesB" (Bayesian variable selection via BGLR). used methods "RM.G" "RM.GE". nIter Integer. title: Joint Genomic Regression Analysis (Reaction Norm Parameters) — jgra
code: # \donttest{ d <- load_crop_data("sorghum") exp_trait <- prepare_trait_data(d$traits, "FTdap") env_mean_trait <- compute_env_means(exp_trait, d$env_meta) params <- c("DL", "GDD", "PTT", "PTR", "PTS") env_mean_trait <- compute_window_params(env_mean_trait, d$env_params, 20, 60, params) pheno <- prepare_line_by_env(exp_trait, env_mean_trait) SNPs <- prepare_genotype(d$genotype, unique(exp_trait$line_code)) geno_df <- data.frame(line_code = rownames(SNPs), SNPs, check.names = FALSE) envir <- env_mean_trait[, c("env_code", "kPara")] result <- jgra(pheno, geno_df, envir, "kPara", d$env_meta,                method = "RM.E", fold = 5, reshuffle = 2) result$r_across #> [1] 0.449978 # }

text: Identifies critical environmental windows covariates driving genotype--environment interaction multi-environment trials. Includes CERIS exhaustive search algorithm, Joint Regression Analysis (JRA), Joint Genomic Regression Analysis (JGRA), cross-validation methods, genomic prediction.
